tweets.text:
Ngl, this came as a big shock to me.

The most decentralized perp DEX in crypto just launched a joint product with @RobinhoodApp.

The dYdX v4 chain shipped everything a decentralization maxi would want, and @HyperliquidX still stole its market share because of speed and UX.

dYdX understood the situation it's in, so it became @arcus_xyz:

→ Joint build with Robinhood Chain
→ 24/7 trading on 95 tokenized stocks + 35 RWA perps
→ 0% trading fees (RFQ execution)
→ Distribution to Robinhood's 25M users

Antonio (dYdX's CEO) said that competitors capture market share by prioritizing speed, simplicity, and liquidity.

He went ahead and said out loud what most DEX founders won't.

The team looked at their situation objectively and understood that if they didn't ship something that competes on speed and UX, they would have kept losing market share.

Rebrands like this are hard, but it's the right move IMO.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
dYdX is now Arcus.

A leading DEX on Robinhood Chain. 

24/7 trading on 95 stock tickers with 0% fees, and liquidity from top institutional market-makers. https://t.co/BQz0WAoTXM

tweets.text:
hyperliquid:native 

3.5x rally 

retest prev ath 

consolidate above those highs 

hype recently increased their revenue a small amount by. 4-6% per day at 100k per day with priority fees. again, small in the idea of a daily number and no impact day to day. you could say the same for when aqav2 goes live with yield. another 500-600k per day. meaningless in daily flows. the impact is always time based. thats 600k per day over 12 months? 

continue to think of it as. You go to the gym, you work out. You look in the mirror. No changes day to day. you can't really see the results even though they are happening 

look again in 3 months, and you see the results. thats how the revenue works at this scale and thats why hype has performed the way it has this year. the short term is just noise.

Here's your weekly snapshot of the best stablecoin yield opportunities listed on Stablewatch ($10M+ in TVL)

Data from @stablewatchHQ ↓

• @UnitasLabs $sUSDu – 15.40% APY
Yield comes from a basket of market-neutral strategies, including funding rate capture, lending, trading fee accrual, and JLP yield capture.

• @noon_capital $sUSN – 12.42% APY
Yield comes from capital deployed across delta-neutral strategies and RWA-backed assets.

• @Main_St_Finance $msY – 12% APY
Yield comes from a delta-neutral options strategy on the CME, with part of the yield directed into an insurance fund.

• @gaib_ai $sAID – 10.61% APY
Yield comes from RWA exposure, including treasuries and AI infrastructure financing, with returns generated from borrower interest payments.

• @PikuFinance $USP – 10.09% APY
Yield comes from underlying USD stablecoins deployed into DeFi strategies and other yield-generating onchain/offchain assets.

• @avantprotocol $savUSD – 7.77% APY
Yield comes from managed strategies that allocate reserves across chains and protocols.

• @infinifilabs $siUSD – 7.72% APY
Yield comes from onchain DeFi operations used by the protocol to generate returns.

• @USDai_Official $sUSDai – 6.92% APY
Yield comes from RWA exposure, including treasuries and AI infrastructure financing, with returns generated from infrastructure borrower interest payments.

• @Neutrl $sNUSD – 6.13% APY
Yield comes from a portfolio of liquid stablecoins, yield-bearing assets, delta-neutral strategies, and hedged OTC positions.

• @Theo_Network $sthUSD – 5.13% APY
Yield comes from a delta-neutral gold carry strategy using tokenized gold exposure, short gold futures, and T-bill collateral.

Which protocols are you using?

tweets.text:
I don't know, but "there is no beef" sounds a lot different to me.

I just saw this and my first instinct was to ignore it. But I think it's important to address because situations like this are exactly how misinformation and false narratives start to spread, and I don't want that to happen.

First, I have nothing but respect for @hosseeb and @tarunchitra. They are genuinely among the few people in this industry who care about pushing it forward, and I've always looked up to their work. Which is exactly why it's disappointing to hear them dunk on someone they've never spoken to and don't actually know anything about.

Now to the substance of what was said:

Tarun made it sound like I promoted some massive staking ponzi or pump and dump scheme to enrich myself. This is simply not true.

Yes, I was very vocal about DeSci. And there is a specific reason for that. Before pivoting my career to crypto, I spent many years in academic cancer research. I know this industry and its pain points inside out. And trust me, there are many. Obviously crypto won't solve all of them, but the nature of tokenization and decentralized capital formation are genuinely well suited to address some of them.

That's why I was excited about it conceptually, and still am. It's also why we made several investments in this space that I talked about publicly. We have sold almost none of these positions, so yes, we are down bad on DeSci. That's part of the venture game and that's totally fine. We always had a long term view on these because, believe it or not, science doesn't get disrupted overnight.

I think it's important to emphasize this because it's probably the assumption behind Tarun's comments. And honestly, it would be a fair concern if it were true. But it isn't.

But maybe, Tarun is right. Maybe I shouldn't have been so loud about it. So if anyone lost money on this, just as we did, I feel genuinely sorry about that. But people who follow me know that this is just my style. I treat my X account as a place to think out loudly and give real insights into my thinking and the Moonrock investment theses. Sometimes that means being very vocal about something early that doesn't work out. But it also means being very vocal about something like Collector when everyone was laughing at it and now it's the entire industry's darling. The toxic nature of CT is that obviously nobody ever focuses on the positive side of things when they can also publicly crucify you instead.

But coming back to DeSci, yes it turned into a massively overhyped, unsustainable pump cycle. Opportunistic people flooded in trying to extract value, and suddenly everyone was a scientist and biotech investor. Classic crypto. So I completely understand why people from the outside might look at it and think it's a scam vertical.

But would we call DePIN a scam vertical because it has broken tokenomics and many projects turned out to be rugs? Obviously not.

The same applies to DeSci. It remains a fascinating vertical with real potential to solve problems I personally experienced and was deeply frustrated by as a researcher. But it's also a vertical that still has many of its own problems to solve and needs time to mature.

I think the biggest issue is just the expectation most crypto investors have. Everyone has been conditioned by memecoin mania and wants to get rich overnight, but that's not how science works. Research has incredibly long feedback cycles. Real breakthroughs take years, sometimes decades. So investors lose interest and move on to the next hot thing.

But that doesn't make it a scam vertical. And it doesn't mean we should put the few brilliant scientists who are genuinely trying to do good for humanity into the same bucket as memecoin pump and dumpers. There's a lot of room between "this cured cancer overnight" and "this was all a scam." The truth, as usual, is somewhere in the middle.

I'd welcome an actual conversation with either of you anytime. My DMs have always been open.

thread_tweets.text:
I don't know, but "there is no beef" sounds a lot different to me.

I just saw this and my first instinct was to ignore it. But I think it's important to address because situations like this are exactly how misinformation and false narratives start to spread, and I don't want that to happen.

First, I have nothing but respect for @hosseeb and @tarunchitra. They are genuinely among the few people in this industry who care about pushing it forward, and I've always looked up to their work. Which is exactly why it's disappointing to hear them dunk on someone they've never spoken to and don't actually know anything about.

Now to the substance of what was said:

Tarun made it sound like I promoted some massive staking ponzi or pump and dump scheme to enrich myself. This is simply not true.

Yes, I was very vocal about DeSci. And there is a specific reason for that. Before pivoting my career to crypto, I spent many years in academic cancer research. I know this industry and its pain points inside out. And trust me, there are many. Obviously crypto won't solve all of them, but the nature of tokenization and decentralized capital formation are genuinely well suited to address some of them.

That's why I was excited about it conceptually, and still am. It's also why we made several investments in this space that I talked about publicly. We have sold almost none of these positions, so yes, we are down bad on DeSci. That's part of the venture game and that's totally fine. We always had a long term view on these because, believe it or not, science doesn't get disrupted overnight.

I think it's important to emphasize this because it's probably the assumption behind Tarun's comments. And honestly, it would be a fair concern if it were true. But it isn't.

But maybe, Tarun is right. Maybe I shouldn't have been so loud about it. So if anyone lost money on this, just as we did, I feel genuinely sorry about that. But people who follow me know that this is just my style. I treat my X account as a place to think out loudly and give real insights into my thinking and the Moonrock investment theses. Sometimes that means being very vocal about something early that doesn't work out. But it also means being very vocal about something like Collector when everyone was laughing at it and now it's the entire industry's darling. The toxic nature of CT is that obviously nobody ever focuses on the positive side of things when they can also publicly crucify you instead.

But coming back to DeSci, yes it turned into a massively overhyped, unsustainable pump cycle. Opportunistic people flooded in trying to extract value, and suddenly everyone was a scientist and biotech investor. Classic crypto. So I completely understand why people from the outside might look at it and think it's a scam vertical.

But would we call DePIN a scam vertical because it has broken tokenomics and many projects turned out to be rugs? Obviously not.

The same applies to DeSci. It remains a fascinating vertical with real potential to solve problems I personally experienced and was deeply frustrated by as a researcher. But it's also a vertical that still has many of its own problems to solve and needs time to mature.

I think the biggest issue is just the expectation most crypto investors have. Everyone has been conditioned by memecoin mania and wants to get rich overnight, but that's not how science works. Research has incredibly long feedback cycles. Real breakthroughs take years, sometimes decades. So investors lose interest and move on to the next hot thing.

But that doesn't make it a scam vertical. And it doesn't mean we should put the few brilliant scientists who are genuinely trying to do good for humanity into the same bucket as memecoin pump and dumpers. There's a lot of room between "this cured cancer overnight" and "this was all a scam." The truth, as usual, is somewhere in the middle.

I'd welcome an actual conversation with either of you anytime. My DMs have always been open.

tweets.text: Someone asked if the US economy could be damaged by the high valuations startups get now, and I realized there's a natural protection against this. All the growth is concentrated in the big hits, and they are exactly the companies most able to grow into an "excessive" valuation.

thread_tweets.text: Someone asked if the US economy could be damaged by the high valuations startups get now, and I realized there's a natural protection against this. All the growth is concentrated in the big hits, and they are exactly the companies most able to grow into an "excessive" valuation.
thread_tweets.full_text: Someone asked if the US economy could be damaged by the high valuations startups get now, and I realized there's a natural protection against this. All the growth is concentrated in the big hits, and they are exactly the companies most able to grow into an "excessive" valuation.

thread_tweets.text: It's certainly true that a startup can be damaged by raising too much and/or at too high a valuation. Raising too much makes you spend too much. And a high valuation increases the chance of a catastrophic down round later on.
thread_tweets.full_text: It's certainly true that a startup can be damaged by raising too much and/or at too high a valuation. Raising too much makes you spend too much. And a high valuation increases the chance of a catastrophic down round later on.

thread_tweets.text: So high valuations can be a problem, definitely. But the startups that matter most for economic growth (and investors' returns) are exactly the ones that high valuations are least likely to damage.
thread_tweets.full_text: So high valuations can be a problem, definitely. But the startups that matter most for economic growth (and investors' returns) are exactly the ones that high valuations are least likely to damage.

tweets.full_text:
Someone asked if the US economy could be damaged by the high valuations startups get now, and I realized there's a natural protection against this. All the growth is concentrated in the big hits, and they are exactly the companies most able to grow into an "excessive" valuation.

It's certainly true that a startup can be damaged by raising too much and/or at too high a valuation. Raising too much makes you spend too much. And a high valuation increases the chance of a catastrophic down round later on.

So high valuations can be a problem, definitely. But the startups that matter most for economic growth (and investors' returns) are exactly the ones that high valuations are least likely to damage.

tweets.text:
When you look back over the last 250 years, the United States has been an amazing place that has given people equal opportunity. People came from all over to work and live well together, becoming prosperous because you could have a good balance of freedom, rule of law, and civility. People were willing to compromise and work together because they recognized that, while imperfect, the greater system was ultimately fair.

Our founding fathers had an aspirational dream of what could be possible, and that dream became a reality. It is one hell of an accomplishment, and we must not lose sight of the principles behind that success.

Which principles do you think have contributed most to America’s success?

#4thOfJuly #America250 #AmericanDream #Principles
tweets.full_text:

People came from all over to work and live well together, becoming prosperous because you could have a good balance of freedom, rule of law, and civility. People were willing to compromise and work together because they recognized that, while imperfect, the greater system was ultimately fair.

Our founding fathers had an aspirational dream of what could be possible, and that dream became a reality. It is one hell of an accomplishment, and we must not lose sight of the principles behind that success.

Which principles do you think have contributed most to America’s success?

#4thOfJuly #America250 #AmericanDream #Principles

tweets.text:
This is the most important chart in all of macro and most people still don't get it.

Labor force participation rate vs government debt as a % of GDP, inverted. They move in perfect lockstep.

Government debt has been accumulated to offset the declining working-age population, and from 2008 onwards, that debt has been mostly just servicing old debts while interest payments keep compounding.

Demographics are destiny and ours are already written. The workers for the next decade have already been born. Or rather, they haven't been.

Which means the borrowing and the debasement aren't going to ease. They're going to accelerate.

thread_tweets.full_text:
one of the mental traps of investing - especially in earliest stages and in public markets - is that price itself contains information

it is increasingly rare to find undervalued companies, information abundance leads to alpha decay, and price is itself the signal 

hard mode 😤

thread_tweets.text:
this has a corollary - companies priced below fair value often continue to trade below fair value because these price signals compound and become an identifying characteristic of the company 

need insane mass x velocity to break out of these slumps
thread_tweets.full_text:
this has a corollary - companies priced below fair value often continue to trade below fair value because these price signals compound and become an identifying characteristic of the company 

need insane mass x velocity to break out of these slumps

thread_tweets.text: this is why market regime shifts catch people offsides. increasingly convinced early stage venture and public equities will continue to trade narratives in tandem, extreme barbell where signal compounds most explosively at the least and most liquid points

tweets.text:
LMAO the opinion of the lead investor in Venice is even worse for $VVV tokenholders than Erik Voorhees denial statements

He basically says that Erik has always been transparent about the fact that $VVV was a memecoin with extra steps and that if tokenholders are not happy about the dual structure, they should sell the token

You can't blame the VC for making sure they invest in the thing that actually has value (the equity) though

Thanks for playing

tweets.text:
I honestly don’t know how you can dislike the hyperscalers here.

If you’re an AI bull: they’re all at the forefront of the biggest AI investment cycle in history.

If you’re an AI bear: it would take only a quarter or two of earnings to effectively eliminate their net debt and return to being free cash flow monsters.

It’s hard to find a scenario where these balance sheets don’t provide tremendous flexibility.

tweets.text: Similar situation at my HS. The lesbian coach of girls varsity thought it would be good for her team to play some random guys not on the boys team. We played them, a state championship contender, and it was a joke. The girls could barely get the ball past half court. Then the coach stopped the game after one of us “boys” dunked over their star player.

tweets.text:
I don't know if Saylor has started selling BTC, but what's obvious is that the sooner he does it, the better.

We need to see how the market reacts once it will price in the fact that Saylor can sell BTC and not only MSTR to pay the dividends and other obligations.

Btw light claims that we don't know who will get fcked between BTC/MSTR/STRC holders, but there's actually no uncertainty about that: all of them.

In the doomsday scenario he's talking about, they will all dump together because they are correlated to the downside.
(On a short-term panic move like last week, we can have STRC/MSTR dump without BTC, but that's clearly not the mega bear scenario light is referring to)
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
light is claiming that Strategy has begun to sell BTC

Any on-chain sleuth to confirm this statement? https://t.co/2dm2RgM3f8
tweets.full_text:
I don't know if Saylor has started selling BTC, but what's obvious is that the sooner he does it, the better.

We need to see how the market reacts once it will price in the fact that Saylor can sell BTC and not only MSTR to pay the dividends and other obligations.

Btw light claims that we don't know who will get fcked between BTC/MSTR/STRC holders, but there's actually no uncertainty about that: all of them.

In the doomsday scenario he's talking about, they will all dump together because they are correlated to the downside.
(On a short-term panic move like last week, we can have STRC/MSTR dump without BTC, but that's clearly not the mega bear scenario light is referring to)

tweets.text:
This is a top 5 interview of the last 5 years.  Congrats to @andrewrsorkin and @seemacnbc.

The reason MSM has sucked for so long was because they can’t have genuinely authentic interviews given the layers of legal liability of most CEOs.

Whatever you want to say about guys like Musk & Karp, they embody the rugged American individualism that actually can have real convos.

Karp talks in defense of the enterprises bitching about LLMs and compute behind closed doors.

Where are the enterprise CEOs with Ballz that will stand by their values like Karp?  Even if you don’t like what Karp stands for, he allows for a real conversation instead of glossed talking points approved by corporate boards.  

Those talking points are the biggest enemy to American values and free speech.  The CEOs know it & the mainstream media knows it. 

That’s why podcast largely win.  It’s an arbitrage of authenticity.  

The people who speak on podcasts aren’t afraid of real convos and their corporate boards.  

Until MSM realizes this, they’ll continue to be have death by 1,000 cuts.

It’s nice to see real journalism tho finally and not corporate cheerleading.  https://t.co/prpqogFlNQ
